items.description_html:
At Data For India, we spend a lot of time on big questions about India: female employment, household spending, fertility, health, the economy, and more. These often require deep research, large datasets, and long-form insights.

But sometimes, you just want a clear answer to one sharp question:
When will India’s population stop growing?
What makes up India’s GDP?
Which diseases affect Indians the most today?

We are now ready to answer those questions for you.

Introducing Data Bytes: one chart about India every week, and the story behind the data.

Every Monday, starting 6th July, we will answer one simple question you might have about India, through one easy-to-understand chart and the context you need to interpret and use the information.

You can already read the first three Data Bytes on our platform, and subscribe now to get the next one in your inbox: https://www.dataforindia.com/data-bytes/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

If there is a question about India you would like us to answer, write to us at info@dataforindia.com.

items.title: This tweet has been coming on my timeline few times now. It's unfortunate, but have three of my learnings on this to share. : 1. Concentrated investin...
items.description_html:
This tweet has been coming on my timeline few times now. It's unfortunate, but have three of my learnings on this to share. :

1. Concentrated investing is a double edged sword - It is not for everyone and doesn't work in 9 out of 10 cases. Most of us would have read stories of wealth creation from concentration, but the truth is that a large part of it is survivorship bias. When you have a super concentrated portfolio, you must be prepared for deep cuts, and frameworks for risk control.

2. Mere cheapness is not a reason to buy - If something is trading "cheap" or below past median valuations, often there is a reason. Growth is the cornerstone of good investing - valuation is secondary. Most investors need better framework of valuations, linked to future and not the past (P/E). Assuming that the market is wrong and you are right is market arrogance.

"It’s pretty hard in a declining business to buy things cheap enough to compensate for the decline."
- Warren Buffett

3. Capacity to suffer - Losses are notional till they are realized. Avoid permanent capital erosion at all costs. Avoid F&O, avoid turnaround bets, avoid investing without manage comfort. All other losses can be recovered. Hence capacity to suffer becomes paramount for investors and also businesses.

Those were my two cents. Happy investing to all.

items.description_html:
IT is up. Everyone is looking for a news or reason. Maybe it's just the realization that pessmism is running ahead of reality (tweeted this yesterday also).

Allied reading:
https://www.ndtvprofit.com/technology/palantir-ceo-karp-slams-anthropic-openai-token-pricing-something-has-gone-completely-wrong-11715871
Strong remarks made by Palantir CEO Alex Karp yesterday. criticized the token-based pricing approach used by AI labs like Anthropic and OpenAI, arguing that “something has gone completely wrong.” He said rising AI costs and newer, pricier model generations are frustrating enterprise customers, leading them to avoid “tokenmaxxing” (spending primarily based on tokens) and instead focus on clearer return on investment.

Karp suggested enterprises may shift toward open-weight models that can deliver similar capabilities at lower cost, and he highlighted concerns that China is advancing rapidly in AI development. He also emphasized a trend away from broad, general-purpose AI and toward more efficient, proprietary tools tailored to specific businesses.

In line with Palantir’s broader stance, Karp positioned open-weight approaches (including Palantir’s work with Nvidia) as a solution—framing the key need for buyers as control over compute, models, and data (the “means of production”), rather than handing that control to external providers.

items.description_html:
RT Archaeological Survey of India
The Dhandi Temple ruins are located at Gohpur in Biswanath, Assam. The temple was commissioned in 1705 CE by Ahom King Rudra Singha. It featured a distinctive octagonal plan on a raised platform and was built using traditional brickwork in lime mortar.

Although the historic structure collapsed during the 1897 Assam earthquake, its remains continue to serve as an important protected archaeological site, preserving the region's rich cultural and structural heritage.

items.title: RT Vikas Saraswat: Why is it so difficult to be a KPS Gill in the system? It is difficult because the overwhelming number of them, be it a bureaucrat,...
items.description_html:
RT Vikas Saraswat
Why is it so difficult to be a KPS Gill in the system? It is difficult because the overwhelming number of them, be it a bureaucrat, a politician, a Cop or even a Judge fear the calumny and ad hominem that might come their way for doing their duty and upholding the integrity of the nation. Tough circumstances demand tough response. When terrorists have breached all bounds of civility and there is a large number cheering, sympathising and sheltering them, the state response cannot remain bound to probity and protocols. When terrorists have complete control over everything from clothing to spoken language, State cannot keep waiting for acts of terror to happen first before getting up and react. It will gear towards preventive action and discretion will be exercised. There will always be a possibility of excesses which in hindsight will constitute human right violations. It requires someone with extraordinary courage and commitment to stand up to the occasion and go for the charge. Such a person knows that he can be hounded by bleeding heart liberals once the embers have cooled. Such a person knows that there will be enough men and women who will curse and abuse him for shattering their secessionist and chauvinist dreams. Such a person knows there will be an Army of virtue signallers who, without knowing the circumstances, would villainise him just to appear cool and righteous. Added to that there is always a threat to life for not only the person but also his kith and kin. And yet this man risks all of it because for him nation and long term peace comes first. Unlike a fence sitting, risk averse, tightrope walker in the system who allows the tactical window to close and the crisis to spiral completely out of control, the call of duty tells this hero that “he who hesitates is lost”. KPS Gill was one such hero. While he was cleaning the mess he would have already anticipated the abuses and insults which would come his way one day. Gill risked ignominy for the sake of righteousness. That makes him an even bigger hero, a giant among pygmies and a worthy son of Mother India.

RT SOIC Research
Today we are going to discuss a very unique 2W OEM that has been able to built a product that is built to last in 10+ years. For context - They had no legacy nothing and have built the business ground up from nothing (product first distribution later)

Ather invested in the things that compound and are hard to copy i.e. design IP, software, brand, charging network and rented the things that don't (cell manufacturing). That is why it spends less than Ola yet has better unit economics, and why the moat is earned rather than bought.

And this is the fruits of investing in building technology and a superior product for years before just thinking about scaling distribution. At the end of the day we can see the result that the product won that did not catch fire :)

But here today we will get into details of how they created this MOAT and how it is now able to win a superior market share and show such a strong growth at the same time when competition did not decrease in fact it has gone up!

items.title: RT Parimal: Next in who after the Ramanujan series? At a tender age of 16, a brilliant but completely unguided boy from Tamil Nadu joined the National...
items.description_html:
RT Parimal
Next in who after the Ramanujan series?

At a tender age of 16, a brilliant but completely unguided boy from Tamil Nadu joined the National Defence Academy & was commissioned into the Indian Navy's electrical branch . He did not have the luxury of a quiet university setting; he was trained in practical skills to maintain weapons systems on warships.

But Arogyaswami Paulraj possessed an insatiable, self-taught obsession with the advanced mathematics of signal processing, control theory & information theory. He studied advanced matrices & random variables by lamplight on naval ships.

By the late 1970s, India faced a serious strategic challenge. After the 1971 Indo-Pak War exposed weaknesses in imported sonars, the Navy needed an advanced anti-submarine warfare system but was blocked by international export restrictions. The Navy turned to Paulraj, then a rising officer with a PhD from IIT Delhi (earned while still in service). He was tasked with leading a major indigenous project to develop a world-class hull-mounted panoramic sonar from scratch.

Operating under intense resource scarcity, Paulraj’s mathematical genius took over. He designed complex signal-processing algos that could filter the chaotic, deafening acoustic noise of the ocean to pinpoint enemy submarines. The resulting system, APSOH (Advanced Panoramic Sonar Hull), inducted in 1983, completely stunned global military observers. It did not just work, it outperformed contemporary Western systems.

After setting up major defense labs in India, Paulraj retired from active naval service & arrived at Stanford University in 1991 as a research associate. This is where the story shifts from military history to modern legend. While working on signal separation experiments for airborne military reconnaissance, Paulraj noticed a strange, fleeting physical phenomenon.

When a radio signal is transmitted in a crowded area (like a city with buildings), it bounces off walls & scatters into 1000s of chaotic, distorted paths. Engineers treated this scattering as a nightmare, multipath interference that corrupted data.

Paulraj had a paradigm-shifting realization rooted in multi-variable calculus & spatial matrices: What if the scattering was not a bug, but a feature?

He realized that if we used multiple antennas at the transmitter & multiple antennas at the receiver, we could use advanced matrix mathematics to isolate those scattered paths & stream parallel, independent channels of data over the exact same frequency, at the exact same time.

He called it MIMO (Multiple Input, Multiple Output).

When he 1st proposed it, the academic world mocked him. Prominent profs & industry skeptics told him it violated the laws of physics & information theory. They claimed it was mathematically impossible to multiply data speeds w/o expanding bandwidth.

Paulraj did no back down. He built his own prototype, founded a startup & proved the mathematics in real-world silicon. He designed the microscopic architecture, the microchip algos that allowed small devices to execute these hyper-complex spatial matrix calculations in fractions of a microsecond.

If we look at the device we are using to read this right now, look at the top corners of our screen. We cannot see them, but embedded inside the frame of our phone are multiple microscopic antennas operating on Paulraj’s exact MIMO-OFDMA mathematics.

Every single modern 4G network, 5G network & high-speed Wi-Fi router on Earth is built entirely on the mathematical foundation invented by the self-taught Indian Navy officer who packed his bags for Stanford. He did not just solve a math problem; he built the invisible highway that carries nearly 100% of the world's mobile data traffic today.

items.title: RT Alter Magazine: An ice cream is a surprisingly elaborate piece of chemistry and physics. Contrary to its name, it isn't just frozen cream. It's fou...
items.description_html:
RT Alter Magazine
An ice cream is a surprisingly elaborate piece of chemistry and physics. Contrary to its name, it isn't just frozen cream.

It's four separate things held together at once:
• ice crystals
• a sugar syrup that never freezes
• air folded in by churning, and
• a network of fat holding it all

Disturb any one of them and the whole thing collapses.

In India, that collapse happens fast.
So Indian manufacturers have started rebuilding ice cream from the inside out. Some are leaning on denser, mithai-style formulations for stability. Others are freezing the product only when it's served. A few are raising protein and prebiotic fibre levels, trading sugar and fat for structure, and a healthier ice cream in the bargain.

This is the story that questions what an ice cream really is, and the strange science being used to combat a ₹10 vanilla cup that gives up by the sixth spoonful.

Issue 5: Designing a Summer Ice Cream for India, written by @chandhana, edited by @fahadmh, scooped, churned & built by @AltCarbonIndia

https://altermag.com/articles/designing-a-summer-ice-cream-for-india

Amber's IL JIN takes a major step toward its next growth phase 👏

Source 👉 Equirus

Key board approvals: • Conversion from Pvt Ltd to Public Ltd • Share split + 25:1 bonus issue • Evaluation of fund raise options, including a potential IPO, rights issue, preferential allotment & debt
Post restructuring, equity capital expands to ~3.54B shares, creating a scalable capital structure for future growth.

Background👉IL JIN, the flagship electronics manufacturing arm of Amber, delivered FY26 revenue of ₹32.7B (~27% of consolidated revenue). Management is targeting ~40% Electronics revenue growth in FY27 with 9.5–10% EBITDA margins.

Equirus says this is significant milestone that strengthens IL JIN's readiness for expansion & potential capital market access.

items.description_html:
Pharma has quietly become one of the strongest-performing sectors over the last 3 months
Nifty Pharma: +15% vs Nifty +0.5% 🤩

Real story is in small-cap pharma, where 27 stocks have delivered more than 2x the returns of the Pharma Index.
🚀 IOL, Bliss GVS & Unichem have surged ~100%.
- Generic exporters are leading the rally, while CDMO, domestic formulations, hospitals and MNC pharma names have largely lagged.

💊 Among large caps, Laurus (+38%), Zydus (+26%) & Mankind (+22%) have stood out.

Sector leadership can often be driven by pockets of the market that receive the least attention 🤔

Source 👉DAM Capital

Explained below
1. Vivo shipments: 22.2mn (CY23) → 25.1mn (CY24) → 29.3mn (CY25), with market share rising 15% → 17% → 19%

2. Management previously indicated the JV could eventually manufacture ~2/3 of Vivo India's volumes = 18–20mn smartphones annually.

3. Dixon produced ~33mn smartphones in FY26. So at full ramp-up, the Vivo JV alone could lift volumes by ~55–60%, reinforcing its leadership in India's smartphone EMS market.

4. FY27 guidance of ~₹56,000cr revenue & 32–33mn smartphone volumes excluded any Vivo contribution.

5. Equirus expects production to begin in 3QFY27, with 8–10mn incremental units in FY27 & a 18–20mn annualized run-rate as the JV scales.

Bottom line: The Vivo JV is a meaningful incremental growth driver beyond Dixon's existing FY27 guidance.

Details below
- Dixon to own 51%; Vivo Mobile India to hold 49%
- GoI has approved Vivo's investment, clearing the way for incorporation
- JV will manufacture Vivo smartphones

CareEdge’s latest sector update points to a clear shift in industry dynamics, where growth is becoming more concentrated, and more competitive.

Key takeaways:

-Retail-led momentum strengthening: Industry premiums grew 8.7% y-o-y in May 2026, driven by health (↑13.7%) and motor segments, with retail health alone expanding 30.5% y-o-y.

-Private insurers & SAHIs gaining share: Private players grew 11.8%, while SAHIs surged 31.7%, pushing their combined market share to 70.9%—highlighting a decisive tilt toward customer-centric, retail-focused models.

-Segment divergence persists: Sharp contractions in fire (-24.5%) and crop insurance continue to offset broader gains, underscoring volatility in commercial and government-linked lines.

items.title: RT Mohammad Ali Shabani: Ali Khamenei began systematically destroying Iranian deterrence when he blinked in January 2020. His crowing about "harsh rev...
items.description_html:
RT Mohammad Ali Shabani
Ali Khamenei began systematically destroying Iranian deterrence when he blinked in January 2020. His crowing about "harsh revenge" for the assassination of Qasem Soleimani turned out to be empty talk. Not long afterwards, top Iranian officials were being killed with such frequency that Iran almost stopped bothering pledging revenge.

Three months into Mojtaba Khamenei's term, killings of Iranians on Iranian soil has become normalized. And by making revenge so central in his discourse, he is mimicking his slain father. If he follows through, he'll be at greater risk. If he doesn't follow through, he might end up like his father.

Whatever the outcome, time will tell if this is some blood feud between Iran and the US/Israel, or between the Khamenei family and Trump/Netanyahu.

items.title: US households are sitting on a massive cash pile despite record equity exposure: Household equity holdings are up to a record ~$72 trillion. This is $...
items.description_html:
US households are sitting on a massive cash pile despite record equity exposure:

Household equity holdings are up to a record ~$72 trillion.

This is $18 trillion, or +33%, above its long-term trend from 2009 to 2019.

Household stock ownership has more than doubled since 2020.

Meanwhile, cash and cash equivalent holdings are up to $20 trillion, an all-time high.

This exceeds the long-term trend by $5 trillion, or +33%.

By comparison, households also own a record $11 trillion in debt securities such as bonds, $2.2 trillion, or +25%, above trend.

American households have never had a bigger cash buffer.

items.title: The market is extremely volatile under the surface: The difference between individual stock volatility in the S&P 500 and the S&P 500 Volatility Index...
items.description_html:
The market is extremely volatile under the surface:

The difference between individual stock volatility in the S&P 500 and the S&P 500 Volatility Index, $VIX, is up to a record 34 points.

This gap has surged +19 points, or +127%, since March.

Over this period, the individual stock volatility index has risen +9 points, to 49 points, the highest since March 2025.

At the same time, $VIX has declined -16 points, to 15 points, the lowest since mid-January.

In other words, the calm in the broader market is hiding unusually high stress across individual stocks.

This comes as the concentration in mega-cap tech continues to keep index volatility artificially low.

Single stocks are trading as if a correction has already occurred.

items.title: Japan's bond market is seeing historic volatility. The 10Y Japanese government bond yield dropped -16 basis points on Friday, to 2.71%, the largest da...
items.description_html:
Japan's bond market is seeing historic volatility.

The 10Y Japanese government bond yield dropped -16 basis points on Friday, to 2.71%, the largest daily decline since April 2025.

This follows a rise to 2.90% on Thursday, the first time the 10Y JGB yield reached that level in 30 years.

At the same time, the 30Y JGB yield fell -13 basis points, to 3.87%, the largest daily drop since January 21st.

This came after Japan's Finance Minister said the government wants to encourage pension funds, including the $1.8 trillion Government Pension Investment Fund, to increase investment in domestic assets.

If this materializes, Japan's largest pension fund could eventually shift allocation away from overseas securities and toward JGBs, easing pressure on the domestic debt market.

The move could also support the Yen by reducing capital outflows from Japan.

Japan’s bond and currency markets are set to remain highly volatile.

items.description_html:
The US economy is now dependent on AI spending:

AI investment now accounts for more than 25% of US GDP growth, the largest contribution on record.

This includes spending on software, IT equipment, R&D, and data centers.

In other words, for every $4 of US economic growth today, over $1 is coming from AI investment.

This comes as AI spending is up to a record ~8% of US GDP.

By comparison, spending on IT equipment, software, and R&D peaked at ~6.5% of GDP during the 2000 Dot-Com bubble.

US economic growth is now all about AI.

items.title: Big tech is resuming market leadership: The proportion of Nasdaq 100 stocks trading at least -20% below their peaks is up to 48%, the highest since th...
items.description_html:
Big tech is resuming market leadership:

The proportion of Nasdaq 100 stocks trading at least -20% below their peaks is up to 48%, the highest since the February-March selloff.

This percentage has doubled over the last 12 months, though it remains below the 60% recorded before the late-March market bottom.

By comparison, during the 2022 bear market, this metric surged to as high as 80%.

This comes despite 64% of Nasdaq 100 stocks still trading above their 200-day moving average, near the highest reading of the year.

To put this into perspective, only 38% of index constituents were trading above their 200-day moving average before the market bottomed on March 30th.

The rally is becoming increasingly dependent on a shrinking group of stocks.

items.title: Iran/US - Is it a blip or a breakdown ? @PramitWorld says that Iran felt betrayed because, despite the ceasefire, potential buyers were not buying the...
items.description_html:
Iran/US - Is it a blip or a breakdown ?

@PramitWorld says that Iran felt betrayed because, despite the ceasefire, potential buyers were not buying their oil due to a lack of "definite clearance" from US Treasury.

10 points from our chat today. Video below.

1. MoU was very vague to start with, a kind of a 'throw-together peace'

2. A clash or flare-up was inevitable at some point

3. Imposing sanctions is easy, undoing them is very difficult

4. Iranian oil were passing via SoH but with no buyers of the oil, due to US sanctions, Iran would have felt betrayed

5. Potential buyers of Iranian oil would have needed extraordinary level of assurance that they are not violating sanctions

6. Iran has pushed the envelope quite a bit w.r.t. SoH, emboldened by Guld states

7. I think the core position of U.S. remains the same - it isn't ready to use military force to reopen the SoH

8. I would imagine Iranian influence in the region would keep growing

9. Trump wants to get out of the war, keep US pump prices low, with a difficult midterms ahead - these reasons have not changed

10. Markets may take the long view that there is enough oil in the world, the Saudis want to pump a lot more & that no one wants to really fight.

items.title: RT Nimesh Shah: Nomura on L&T Fin Buy, TP Rs 370 1QFY27 net profit beat our estimate by 8%; asset-quality trends encouraging Encouraging asset-quality...
items.description_html:
RT Nimesh Shah
Nomura on L&T Fin
Buy, TP Rs 370
1QFY27 net profit beat our estimate by 8%; asset-quality trends encouraging
Encouraging asset-quality trends, especially 2W segment with AI engines in place
Risks – Monsoon and upcoming insurance distribution reform
Lift FY27-29F EPS estimates by 2-10%

JPM on L&T Fin
Neutral, TP Rs 320
L&T Finance reported a strong 1QFY27 with PPoP at Rs 20.2bn (+35% y/y) & PAT at Rs 9.02bn (+29% y/y, +12% q/q) both coming 4% ahead of JPMe.
While NII at Rs 26.8bn (+31% y/y, +5% q/q) came 1% lower vs JPMe on lower NIMs, adverse impact was more than offset by
(i) higher fee income which rose by 22.7% y/y to Rs 6bn (+12% vs JPMe),
(ii) lower opex (-2.6% vs JPMe). NIM (on AUM) declined 10bps q/q (-9bps vs JPMe) which is likely attributable to the LTF maintaining a higher liquidity buffer during the quarter on account of geopolitical uncertainty
On Asset Quality, while GS2+GS3 declined by 18bps q/q to 4.06% (-18bps q/q, -16bps vs JPMe), loan losses at Rs 8bn came 2% above JPMe with credit costs coming 5bps higher vs JPMe at 2.55% (-6bps q/q) primarily driven by a 111/175bps q/q rise in Stage-2/Stage-3 PCR.
Net slippages (change in GS-3+write-offs) rose by 17bps q/q to 2.14%
While there is an element of seasonality, AQ trends will be closely watched especially given the concerns around monsoons
Reported RoA of 2.48% rose 8bps q/q and came 13bps ahead of JPMe.
AUM growth of 27% y/y was retail-led with retail disbursements at Rs 238.5bn (+36% y/y, -1% q/q).
LTF is up 23% in last 1M (vs NIFTY +2.5%), with valuations expanding to 2.3x FY28E PB for 14% RoE.
This likely limits the scope for further re-rating

Now what I can gauge is that there is a clear focus on window profiles and doors as well , kisan integration is also happened on ground and things seem to be improving

One reason the stock has done well is that Mr Sanjay Gupta ( APL Apollo Tubes ) has joined in the management to drive incremental growth here and is usually very agressive and focused on growing the business very well

What we have also seen is the stabilsiation in the cycle and resin prices , I think december was the bottom , after which we saw major spikes in the market doubling from the lows and all over the place , currently prices are trading between 80-85 , and demand trends are stable on the ground for the pipes side clearly showing some improvement from the bottom

For them now it is all about incremental execution and a shift towards market share gains and growing and not protecting margins , it is going to be very interesting to see how Sanjay Gupta drives the growth here now

items.title: RT ValueEquity: I want to Compete with the opportunity set , I am an individual investor and as even Stan druckenmiller says , I actually want to comp...
items.description_html:
RT ValueEquity
I want to Compete with the opportunity set , I am an individual investor and as even Stan druckenmiller says , I actually want to compete with the best performing stocks for the three year period across segments , and that would mean the process based reward for me

Finding the 30-40% best performing themes of a 3 year cycle is what I try to accomplish , as an Individual investor it is less about alpha but scaling multifold Compete with opportunity set of best companies for incremental returns , your returns paradigm will change and that's when you will stop playing the small game

For allocators 2% alpha would mean a lot on multi thousand crore AUM , and yes it would mean a lot for us also , but not as much as scaling multifold by actually trying to capture the big winners My outcomes are compared to with the depth of my coverage universe , was I able to participate in the 3-4 stocks out of the 10 best performing in the 2 year cycle runway , that gives me the edge to scale at an Individual investor level!

But to do this , and something I am constantly working on is the ability to have a broader and deeper coverage for themes with just the right agility to execute when there is opportunity , execution backed by coverage depth with margin of safety is what I keep trying to focus on ! I too am learning to sharpen the edge with time !

items.title: Auto Ancillaries: Premiumisation Driving Higher Content Per Vehicle Rising consumer demand for premium features is increasing the value of components ...
items.description_html:
Auto Ancillaries: Premiumisation Driving Higher Content Per Vehicle

Rising consumer demand for premium features is increasing the value of components per vehicle, creating a structural growth driver for auto ancillary companies.
Connected features are expected to see the fastest adoption, rising from 2% (FY19) to 50% (FY27E).
Sunroof penetration is projected to increase from 10% to 40% over FY19–FY27E.
Automatic transmission (AT) adoption is expected to grow from 17% to 35%, while alloy wheels rise to 50% penetration.
LED lighting penetration is also expected to increase from 20% to 35% by FY27E.
Higher feature content translates into higher vehicle ASPs and greater revenue opportunity for component manufacturers, even if overall vehicle volumes grow at a moderate pace.

items.title: Indo Borax: ₹20 Cr Boron Oxide Capex to Move Up the Value Chain Indo Borax is building its next phase of growth around 4 clear priorities — Boric Ac...
items.description_html:
Indo Borax: ₹20 Cr Boron Oxide Capex to Move Up the Value Chain

Indo Borax is building its next phase of growth around 4 clear priorities — Boric Acid leadership, Boron Oxide expansion, exports and R&D.

- Company will continue to strengthen its leadership in Boric Acid

- Focus remains on capacity enhancement and operational improvements

- Indo Borax is investing ~₹20 crore in a new Boron Oxide facility at Pithampur

- Planned Boron Oxide capacity: 4,000 MTPA

- This will help the company move into higher-margin Boron chemistry products

- International expansion is another key priority

- Focus markets include South America and South-East Asia

- R&D investments will be increased for specialised boron-based solutions

- Target applications include advanced materials, flame retardants and specialty agriculture

Key takeaway:

Indo Borax is trying to evolve from a Boric Acid-led company into a broader Boron chemistry platform.

The ₹20 crore Boron Oxide capex is small in size, but strategically important — it pushes the company towards higher-margin products and newer global markets.

An import substitution play on new and emerging chemical products - Expanding Products and Integration

Going through a strategic transformation

items.description_html:
SK Hynix’s US listing has received a blockbuster response

It raised $26.5 Bn through the largest ever US initial share offering by a foreign company

- 177.9 Mn ADRs issued at $149 each
- The issue was subscribed more than 7x
- Each 10 ADRs represent one common share

But this is not a conventional IPO

SK Hynix was already listed in South Korea. The ADR listing provides direct access to the much deeper US institutional investor base while raising fresh capital for capacity expansion

Despite the stock rising over 600% over the last year, earnings growth has been even stronger. This has resulted in its 12 month forward P/E declining from 7.9x to 5.5x

SK Hynix trades at 5.5x fwd earnings compared with around 6.7x for Micron. Direct access to US investors could help reduce this valuation gap & partially address the Korea discount

Stock has already corrected 25% from its recent peak, while concerns around slower AI spending, aggressive capacity additions and a potential memory downcycle remain. The semiconductor trade has also become extremely crowded.

Overall, a blockbuster response for SK Hynix

items.title: RT Equity Insights Elite: Recency bias is one of the most common mistakes investors make during volatile market phases It happens when we start believ...
items.description_html:
RT Equity Insights Elite
Recency bias is one of the most common mistakes investors make during volatile market phases

It happens when we start believing that recent events will continue forever

When markets fall, investors begin to feel that markets will keep falling

When markets rally, investors assume the rally will continue indefinitely

We saw this clearly during 2020. Sentiment was extremely negative during the lockdown period, yet markets bottomed out & delivered one of the strongest bull runs thereafter

Some of the best opportunities in equities historically have emerged when sentiment was the weakest.

items.title: RT Reads with Ravi: Umberto Eco, who owned 50,000 books, had this to say about home libraries: “It is foolish to think that you have to read all the ...
items.description_html:
RT Reads with Ravi
Umberto Eco, who owned 50,000 books, had this to say about home libraries:

“It is foolish to think that you have to read all the books you buy, as it is foolish to criticize those who buy more books than they will ever be able to read. It would be like saying that you should use all the cutlery or glasses or screwdrivers or drill bits you bought before buying new ones.

“There are things in life that we need to always have plenty of supplies, even if we will only use a small portion.

“If, for example, we consider books as medicine, we understand that it is good to have many at home rather than a few: when you want to feel better, then you go to the ‘medicine closet’ and choose a book. Not a random one, but the right book for that moment. That’s why you should always have a nutrition choice!

“Those who buy only one book, read only that one and then get rid of it. They simply apply the consumer mentality to books, that is, they consider them a consumer product, a good. Those who love books know that a book is anything but a commodity.”

items.title: RT Sann: His name is Dinesh Thakur. He gave up a safe, high paying job and spent eight years of his life fighting one of the largest drug companies in...
items.description_html:
RT Sann
His name is Dinesh Thakur.

He gave up a safe, high paying job and spent eight years of his life fighting one of the largest drug companies in the country. He did it because he discovered that the medicines millions of people trusted were built on a lie.

He was a chemical engineer who had worked in America and come back to India for a senior role at Ranbaxy, then the biggest generic medicine maker in the country. It was a proud Indian company whose cheap medicines were sold across the world.

Not long after he joined, he was asked to look into the quality of the company's drugs. What he found frightened him.

The company was faking its test results. Medicines were being sold without being properly tested for whether they were safe, or whether they even worked. The data handed to regulators was simply invented.

These were not luxury goods. These were the tablets that sick people swallowed every day, trusting that they would heal them.

Thakur went to his own bosses with what he had found. Nothing changed. So he made a choice that cost him everything. He resigned, and he reported the company to the American drug regulators.

Then began eight long years. He handed over evidence, sat through investigation after investigation, and lived under the shadow of taking on a giant that had every reason to crush him.

In 2013, it ended. Ranbaxy admitted its guilt in an American court, pleaded guilty to seven serious crimes, and was made to pay one of the largest fines ever handed to a generic drug maker, five hundred million dollars.

Because one man refused to look away, the way the world checks the safety of its medicines was changed for good.

He had a comfortable life and a good salary. He gave it all up so that the medicine in a stranger's hand could be trusted.

items.title: RT The Seeker: It's a shame no major film has been made about Biju Patnaik. He lived a life that sounds almost fictional: In 1938, he flew his entire ...
items.description_html:
RT The Seeker
It's a shame no major film has been made about Biju Patnaik. He lived a life that sounds almost fictional:

In 1938, he flew his entire wedding procession by aircraft to Rawalpindi for his marriage to Gyanwati Sethi (who became the first Indian woman to hold a commercial pilot’s license, and the couple later flew daring missions together.)

While serving in the Royal Indian Air Force (including as head of Air Transport Command), he secretly ferried freedom fighters like Jayaprakash Narayan, Ram Manohar Lohia, and Aruna Asaf Ali to their hideouts. He also airdropped 'Quit India' leaflets to Indian troops while evacuating British families.

The British once jailed him for transporting nationalist leaders and distributing anti-British literature but also honored him for bravery in evacuating civilians from Japanese-occupied Burma.

During WWII, he flew daring missions delivering arms and supplies to Soviet forces in Stalingrad, and over the treacherous 'Hump' route to China in
support of Chinese Nationalist forces against the Japanese.

In July 1947, he and his wife flew a Dakota into Indonesia, landed on an improvised airstrip while evading Dutch anti-aircraft fire, rescued Prime Minister Sutan Sjahrir, refuelled using abandoned Japanese fuel, and flew him safely to India via Singapore.

A couple of months later, during the Pakistani invasion of Kashmir, he airlifted one of the first Indian troops into Srinagar.

The same year he founded Kalinga Airlines, one of India's earliest private airlines.

As Odisha CM post-1962 India-China war, he secretly visited CIA headquarters at Langley to forge a covert India-US partnership against China, which led to the Aviation Research Centre (ARC) at Charbatia, which supported U-2 recon missions over Tibet.

He was also a legendary administrator and statesman, laying much of the foundation of Odisha's industrial empire (mines, ports, heavy industries etc).

When he passed away, Indonesia observed 7 days of state mourning. Russia observed 1 day. He remains the only person in Indian history whose body was draped in the national flags of 3 nations - India, Indonesia, and Russia.

Few public figures have left such a footprint.

His extraordinary life needs to be brought to the big screen.


Shiv Aroor: What event in Indian history/recent current affairs do you want to see a film made on?

items.title: RT Mayor Zohran Kwame Mamdani: Today marks 250 years since a small group of newspaper editors, farmers, and soldiers signed a document declaring our n...
items.description_html:
RT Mayor Zohran Kwame Mamdani
Today marks 250 years since a small group of newspaper editors, farmers, and soldiers signed a document declaring our nation’s independence — a truth that feels self-evident now but was revolutionary then.

What a privilege us Americans have, to live in a nation that every one of its inhabitants can shape. What an honor us New Yorkers have, to look out over our city’s waters from the shores where so many Americans bravely entered their country for the first time.

Today and all days, let us remember that patriotism is not pretending our nation is without flaws. Patriotism is every act of righteous dissent — because loving our country means fighting for the best version of it.

Happy Fourth of July, New York City.

items.title: RT Jesús Fernández-Villaverde: This is a photo I took of Keynes’s bedroom at Charleston House, Vanessa Bell and Duncan Grant’s farmhouse. Keynes w...
items.description_html:
RT Jesús Fernández-Villaverde
This is a photo I took of Keynes’s bedroom at Charleston House, Vanessa Bell and Duncan Grant’s farmhouse. Keynes wrote The Economic Consequences of the Peace at this table, looking out over the pond (also notice Duncan Grant’s 1917 portrait of Keynes in the top left corner, painted in the garden).

Keynes liked the area so much that he later leased Tilton House, just down the lane.

As always, Keynes was a dominating figure. Rumor has it that he changed all the clocks in the house to his own eccentric time zone, “Charleston time,” which suited the household’s bohemian lifestyle. Whether this is true is hard to ascertain, but it fits the atmosphere quite well.

It was most interesting for me to be in the room where a book I teach every fall semester came to life. The curator, however, was intrigued by the fact that I seemed to care far more about Keynes than about Duncan Grant or Vanessa Bell. I apologized to her for my professional bias, pointing out that nobody is perfect!

items.title: RT Sumita Kale: Very important change that has been observed- time to go way beyond averages. Unless we appreciate what has changed, how will our urba...
items.description_html:
RT Sumita Kale
Very important change that has been observed- time to go way beyond averages. Unless we appreciate what has changed, how will our urban plans and models adapt?

"The altostratus clouds, which were once associated with the Indian monsoon, have become less common over the Indian territory, especially over Delhi-NCR. These clouds generally bring small quantities of rain over a prolonged period.
Such clouds have been replaced by cumulonimbus clouds, also known as thunderstorms, which are characterised by intense rain spells, lightning, hail, and strong winds."

"A senior official from the India Meteorological Department told ThePrint that climate change and extreme urbanisation are likely among the many factors behind this shifting monsoon trend and cloud formation patterns.

“Rainfall data confirms this trend. Over the last few years, we have been observing that rainfall that we used to get in a staggered period over the entire season is received in a few spells. The rest of the season remains dry,” the official said, adding that such a shift is neither suitable for urban centres nor conducive to agricultural activities."

Would be grateful for links to more research on this @RockSea @Hosalikar_KS @vineet_mausam Thanks!
@rapiduplift @sushmadate
https://theprint.in/environment/monsoon-indian-clouds-climate-change/2978458/

items.title: RT Pratik Bhakta: UK-based cross-border remittance platform Aspora, which uses stable coins to transfer funds across international markets has picked ...
items.description_html:
RT Pratik Bhakta
UK-based cross-border remittance platform Aspora, which uses stable coins to transfer funds across international markets has picked up a 2% stake in Shivalik Small Finance Bank.

The fintech invested Rs 34crore for a 1.99% stake valuing the small finance bank at Rs 1,900 crore.

Interestingly the deal happened in February this year, but has so far gone under the radar.

Aspora, founded in Bengaluru but now headquartered in London, is backed by Sequoia US, Greylock, YCombinator and others.

Notable development given Aspora uses stable coin rails to transfer funds across borders faster and at cheaper rates. Last year the company claimed that its 250K users saved $15mn in fees by using crypto rails for cross-border remittance.

In India traditional financiers have mostly stayed away from crypto because of lack of regulations. Additionally tax authorities are understood to be scrutinising stablecoin-based remittance payments.

items.title: RT Moses Harding John: Now that I am seeing the Life & General Insurance businesses closely, I notice that distributors walk away with big chunk of th...
items.description_html:
RT Moses Harding John
Now that I am seeing the Life & General Insurance businesses closely, I notice that distributors walk away with big chunk of the cake while the product manufacturers struggle to “contain” claim settlements (adjusted for reinsurance) & operating costs to stay in profit after adjusting the losses from core business against income from investment portfolio!

Cost of acquisition (distribution fees) + reinsurance + claim settlements + operating cost is always >100% net premium (gross premium adjusted for distribution fees + reinsurance) which is a funny business model! The profitability is high dependent on the performance of the investment portfolio, like an AMC!

Banks owning LI & GI (that includes health) businesses subsidize the cost of acquisition through their branch network to stay ahead in the race!

IRDAI needs to set up a fair play between the companies & distributors to make the insurance sector thrive & be customer efficient!

Coverage of risks around life & health and movable & immovable assets is as critical as banking & financial services sector, but unfortunately not provided the desired regulatory attention beyond governance & compliance!

Insurance sector is like a (life & non-life) risk-underwriting AMC!

items.title: The AI Trade's Quietest Chokepoint Isn't a Chip ABF substrate TAM is set to compound at 33% through 2028, reaching roughly $18bn, with ROE heading to ...
items.description_html:
The AI Trade's Quietest Chokepoint Isn't a Chip

ABF substrate TAM is set to compound at 33% through 2028, reaching roughly $18bn, with ROE heading to a fresh record near 32%. The GPU can't ship without this film. (Goldman Sachs)

Our View

India localised the assembly floor but left the substrate layer, the exact thing this upcycle is about, almost entirely to Japan and Taiwan. A 33% CAGR in AI substrates makes that gap expensive to keep ignoring. The honest catch is that organic ABF is a twenty-year craft India cannot copy overnight, so the realistic entry is one of two doors: localise substrate as a mandated supplier feeding domestic OSAT, or leapfrog into glass-core substrate, where the whole industry is still early and the head start is measured in quarters, not decades. Breaks if ISM keeps funding assembly while leaving substrate to imports, in which case India stays a packaging floor with the valuable film sourced abroad.

items.title: India's Boring Balance Sheet Is the Feature, Not the Bug India-first flip. India's sub-50% credit ratio looks unambitious next to Vietnam, until you r...
items.description_html:
India's Boring Balance Sheet Is the Feature, Not the Bug

India-first flip. India's sub-50% credit ratio looks unambitious next to Vietnam, until you remember that under-leverage is what gives you room to run when the cycle turns.

Our take :

The eye goes to Vietnam, but the quiet signal is India's own restraint. India's private credit to GDP sits near the bottom of this pack, well under half Vietnam's level, and that gap is usually read as a weakness. Flip it. Low leverage is dry powder. It means India's credit cycle still has room to expand for years before it looks anything like Vietnam's, which supports a longer runway for lenders and credit-sensitive demand at home.

items.title: Japan Runs Out of People, One Industry at a Time Autos must triple their foreign workforce share to 27% by 2040 just to hold output flat. Farm product...
items.description_html:
Japan Runs Out of People, One Industry at a Time

Autos must triple their foreign workforce share to 27% by 2040 just to hold output flat. Farm production value halves by 2050. A third of domestic freight goes unshipped by 2030. This is not one labour shortage. It is an economy running down its headcount sector by sector.

The shortage that automation can't paper over. Japan already runs more than three times the global robot density and it has not solved the labour gap, because the missing workers are drivers, farmers and carers, not assembly-line hands. The read is that the demographic squeeze is a services and blue-collar problem, and capex on factory robots is the wrong tool for most of it.

Shocking stat of the day:

Nvidia, $NVDA, Micron, $MU, Broadcom, $AVGO, and Applied Materials, $AMAT, are now expected to generate a record $430 billion in combined free cash flow (FCF) over the next 12 months.

That would be more than TRIPLE the FCF they generated just 2 years ago.

At the same time, the combined FCF of Amazon, $AMZN, Alphabet, $GOOGL, Meta, $META, Microsoft, $MSFT, and Oracle, $ORCL, is projected to turn negative for the first time on record.

That would mark a massive reversal from the +$260 billion peak reported by these companies in 2024.

This comes as AI-related CapEx by these 5 companies is estimated to surge to ~$1.8 trillion in 2026 and 2027 combined.

Chipmakers are becoming cash machines, while AI giants are burning record amounts of capital.

items.title: RT Mickey from Arcadia: The 10 craziest parts of the ChatGPT lawsuit. Apple says: 1.) A former Apple engineer named Chang Liu quit Apple in January 20...
items.description_html:
RT Mickey from Arcadia
The 10 craziest parts of the ChatGPT lawsuit.

Apple says:

1.) A former Apple engineer named Chang Liu quit Apple in January 2026 to join OpenAI, secretly kept his Apple-issued laptop instead of returning it, then discovered a bug that let him log back into Apple's private servers from his new desk at OpenAI.

He didn't report the bug. He didn't log off. He texted a friend still working at Apple: "LOL, I found out I can access the [server], so funny." Then he spent weeks downloading over 1,000 pages of Apple's confidential engineering files while actively building competing hardware for OpenAI.

2.) Apple is suing OpenAI's Chief Hardware Officer a man named Tang Yew Tan who spent 24 years at Apple as VP of Product Design for iPhone and Apple Watch before leaving to co-found OpenAI's hardware division.

Apple alleges that when Tan interviewed Apple employees for jobs at OpenAI, he told them to physically bring Apple hardware to the interview. Batteries. Circuit boards. Logic boards. Prototype parts. Actual physical components taken from Apple's facilities.

One candidate was so caught off guard he said he "didn't even know we could take those from the office."

3.) Apple's lawsuit reveals that OpenAI was circulating a confidential internal Apple document, one marked "Need to Know," intended only for Apple's own managers, among new OpenAI hires.

The document details Apple's security procedures for when an employee resigns.

OpenAI was sharing it with recruits before they even told Apple they were leaving, so they knew exactly what checks to expect and how to avoid them. OpenAI employees referred to it as "a checklist that Tang put together" even though it was clearly an Apple internal document.

4.) Apple's investigation found that OpenAI was actively coaching Apple employees on how to handle their exit from Apple.

The advice included: don't sign anything at your exit interview. And if Apple asks you to sign something, contact OpenAI immediately. OpenAI was running legal interference on Apple's own offboarding process in real time, from the inside.

5.) Tang Tan, the former Apple VP who is now OpenAI's Chief Hardware Officer, was reportedly warning Apple employees he was recruiting not to tell Apple they had accepted jobs at OpenAI.

The strategy: stay at Apple as long as possible, keep your badge and system access active, and keep gathering information.

Apple says it found a clear pattern of departing employees skipping exit interviews, ignoring security outreach, and taking steps to evade the procedures designed to protect Apple's confidential data.

6.) OpenAI's interview process for hardware candidates, many of them current Apple employees, required them to prepare a "Technical Deep Dive" presentation with slides about their work.

The instructions asked candidates to disclose which vendors Apple uses, how Apple selects hardware components, what software tools Apple uses for system integration, and how Apple manages its supplier relationships.

7.) Apple's lawsuit alleges that OpenAI interviewers, former Apple insiders who knew the company's internal language, were using Apple's secret project code names during job interviews to ask candidates about unreleased Apple products.

Apple documented at least one case where a candidate began screenshotting and downloading confidential Apple files in the hours before his OpenAI interview.

During the interview, Tang Tan asked him about the exact same project. Apple says this has become an established pattern.

8.) Apple alleges that OpenAI, through its hardware subsidiary io Products, approached one of Apple's trusted industrial design partners and had them perform Apple's proprietary metal-finishing technique the multi-step process Apple developed over years to produce the distinctive look and feel of its products.

OpenAI told the partner that Apple had given permission. Apple had given no such permission. The partner was bound by exclusivity agreements with Apple that explicitly prohibited doing this work for anyone else.

9.) The engineer who broke back into Apple's servers, Chang Liu wasn't acting alone. Apple's lawsuit alleges he was simultaneously coaching a current Apple employee named Alyssa Peng on how to copy files from Apple workstations without triggering the security team, directing her to specific confidential project folders, and using Apple's stolen data to help her prepare for her own OpenAI job interview.

To keep all of this hidden, Liu told Peng to stop communicating over Apple devices and switch to a separate private messaging app called LINE.

10.) Apple's lawsuit frames this not as the actions of a few rogue employees, but as a deliberate institutional strategy. OpenAI now employs over 400 former Apple engineers and executives.

Apple alleges that OpenAI built its entire hardware recruiting pipeline around extracting Apple's proprietary knowledge from manufacturing processes and supplier relationships to unreleased product designs and that as a result, OpenAI's nascent hardware business is, in Apple's words, "rotten to its core by its illegal reliance on misappropriated trade secrets."

items.title: RT Film Updates: Christopher Nolan on AI: “I’ve never seen a more rapid wholesale dismissal of a supposedly foundational jump in technology in my li...
items.description_html:
RT Film Updates
Christopher Nolan on AI:

“I’ve never seen a more rapid wholesale dismissal of a supposedly foundational jump in technology in my lifetime. So much energy has been expended on bringing in AI, but if you look at that generation’s reaction, they’re utterly rejecting it. [My children’s] judgment of AI slop has been immediate and harsh. They see it for what it is very quickly – and it’s much easier for them to identify it, because it grew out of an online world they know really well. And while that doesn’t mean that every aspect of the technology is useless or meaningless, in film-making it’s hitting at exactly the wrong time. After years of driving towards heavily virtual environments, we’re seeing a renewed interest in more tactile, more real forms of storytelling.”

🔗 https://www.telegraph.co.uk/films/2026/07/10/christopher-nolan-interview-odyssey-backlash/
